origin: A line from Christian scripture (the New Testament, Gospel of John), functioning as a concluding encouragement/discourse in which Jesus speaks to his disciples near the time of his departure.
author: Traditionally attributed to John the Evangelist (authorship of the Gospel of John is generally considered anonymous but associated with John’s community). In the text itself, the speaker is presented as Jesus.
thoughts: The author/speaker’s motivation appears pastoral and exhortative: to reassure believers that suffering (“tribulation” in the world) is real but not final, and to ground peace and courage in the confidence that Jesus has already “overcome the world.” The perspective is one of spiritual authority and consolatory certainty rather than ordinary human optimism.
audience: Early Christian believers (disciples and the wider community) who are experiencing or anticipating hardship; likely a readership trying to remain faithful amid social pressure, persecution, or internal uncertainty.
written_when: Late 1st century CE, commonly dated roughly to about 90–110 CE (after the destruction of the Jerusalem temple in 70 CE).
